questions.question_text: A class of 40 students is surveyed. 16 students prefer blue. What is the relative frequency of students who prefer blue?
questions.explanation: Relative frequency = 16/40 = 0.40, or 40%.

questions.question_text: In a two-way table with a grand total of 200, the joint frequency for 'Male & Agrees' is 50. What is the joint relative frequency?
questions.explanation: Joint relative frequency = 50/200 = 0.25 = 25%.

questions.question_text: A two-way table shows: A & X = 30, A & Y = 20, B & X = 25, B & Y = 25. What is the marginal relative frequency for row A?
questions.explanation: Row A total = 30 + 20 = 50. Grand total = 30 + 20 + 25 + 25 = 100. Marginal relative frequency = 50/100 = 0.50.

questions.question_text: Out of 80 surveyed people, 20 are male and prefer coffee. What is this joint relative frequency expressed as a percentage?
questions.explanation: Joint relative frequency = 20/80 = 0.25 = 25%.

questions.question_text: A table has the following conditional relative frequencies for column X: A & X = 60%, B & X = 40%. There are 50 items in column X. How many are in cell A & X?
questions.explanation: 60% of 50 = 0.60 x 50 = 30 items in cell A & X.

questions.question_text: If all joint relative frequencies in a two-way table are added together, what should they equal?

options.text: It depends on the data

questions.explanation: All joint relative frequencies represent parts of the whole, so when added together they equal 1 (or 100%).

questions.question_text: In a two-way table, 45 out of 60 females chose option A. What is the conditional relative frequency of choosing option A given the person is female?
questions.explanation: Conditional relative frequency = 45/60 = 0.75, or 75% of females chose option A.

questions.question_text: A two-way table has a grand total of 500. The marginal frequency for 'Disagree' is 150. What is the marginal relative frequency for 'Disagree'?
questions.explanation: Marginal relative frequency = 150/500 = 0.30, or 30%.

questions.question_text: If the conditional relative frequency of passing a test given tutoring is 0.85, and 60 students received tutoring, how many passed?
questions.explanation: Number who passed = 0.85 x 60 = 51 students.

questions.question_text: A relative frequency table shows these joint relative frequencies: 0.15, 0.25, 0.35, and one unknown value. What is the missing value?
questions.explanation: All joint relative frequencies must sum to 1. So the missing value = 1 - 0.15 - 0.25 - 0.35 = 0.25.

questions.question_text: What is 'relative frequency'?

options.text: The raw count of data in a category
The ratio of a frequency to the total number of observations, expressed as a fraction, decimal, or percentage
The difference between two frequencies
The number of categories in a data set

questions.explanation: Relative frequency is the proportion of a category's count to the total number of observations. It can be expressed as a fraction, decimal, or percentage.

questions.question_text: What is 'joint relative frequency'?

options.text: The relative frequency of a row or column total compared to the grand total
The ratio of a cell's frequency to the grand total of the table
The frequency of one variable without considering the other
The correlation between two variables

questions.explanation: Joint relative frequency is the ratio of a specific cell's count to the grand total, representing the proportion of all observations that fall in that particular combination of categories.

questions.question_text: What is 'marginal relative frequency'?

options.text: The ratio of a cell's count to the grand total
The ratio of a row or column total to the grand total
The frequency within a single condition
The largest frequency in the table

questions.explanation: Marginal relative frequency is the proportion of a row total or column total relative to the grand total. It summarizes the distribution of one variable regardless of the other.

questions.question_text: What is 'conditional relative frequency'?

options.text: The relative frequency of one category within a specific group or condition of another variable
The ratio of the grand total to a single cell
The total of all relative frequencies in a table
The frequency that never changes

questions.explanation: Conditional relative frequency is the proportion of a cell's count relative to its row or column total, showing the frequency of one variable given a specific category of the other.

questions.question_text: What does 'proportion' mean in statistics?

options.text: The total count of observations
A part or share of the whole, expressed as a fraction, decimal, or percentage
The range of the data
The difference between the maximum and minimum values

questions.explanation: A proportion represents a part of the whole and can be expressed as a fraction, decimal, or percentage. Relative frequencies are proportions.

questions.question_text: What does 'distribution' mean when describing data in a table?

options.text: How data values are spread across different categories or values
The single most common value in the data set
The average of all data points
The number of rows in the table

questions.explanation: A distribution describes how data values are spread or allocated across the different categories or possible values, showing the pattern of the data.

questions.question_text: What does 'two-way relative frequency table' mean?

options.text: A two-way table where all counts have been converted to relative frequencies
A table with only two columns
A table showing only marginal totals
A table that uses bar graphs instead of numbers

questions.explanation: A two-way relative frequency table replaces raw frequency counts with proportions (relative frequencies), either as fractions, decimals, or percentages.

questions.question_text: What does 'row conditional relative frequency' mean?

options.text: The cell frequency divided by the grand total
The cell frequency divided by its row total
The row total divided by the column total
The grand total divided by the number of rows

questions.explanation: Row conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a cell's count by the total for its row, showing the proportion within that specific row category.

questions.question_text: What does 'column conditional relative frequency' mean?

options.text: The cell frequency divided by the grand total
The row total divided by the grand total
The cell frequency divided by its column total
The column total divided by the number of cells

questions.explanation: Column conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a cell's count by the total for its column, showing the proportion within that specific column category.

questions.question_text: What does it mean when two conditional relative frequency distributions are nearly identical across rows?

options.text: The two variables are strongly associated
The two variables appear to have no association; they may be independent
There is a computation error in the table
The sample size is too small

questions.explanation: If the conditional distributions are nearly the same across different categories, it suggests the variables are independent and have no meaningful association.

questions.question_text: A high school surveys 250 students about favorite subject (Math or English) and grade level (Junior or Senior). Results: Junior & Math = 60, Junior & English = 50, Senior & Math = 70, Senior & English = 70. What is the joint relative frequency of Senior & Math?
questions.explanation: Joint relative frequency of Senior & Math = 70/250 = 0.28.

questions.question_text: A hospital surveys 400 patients about satisfaction (satisfied or dissatisfied) and department (ER or outpatient). Results: ER & Satisfied = 80, ER & Dissatisfied = 120, Outpatient & Satisfied = 150, Outpatient & Dissatisfied = 50. What is the conditional relative frequency of being satisfied given the patient visited the ER?
questions.explanation: ER total = 80 + 120 = 200. Conditional relative frequency = 80/200 = 0.40 = 40%.

questions.question_text: A store tracks customer purchases by product type (electronics or clothing) and customer type (new or returning). Results: New & Electronics = 45, New & Clothing = 30, Returning & Electronics = 55, Returning & Clothing = 70. What is the marginal relative frequency for returning customers?
questions.explanation: Grand total = 45 + 30 + 55 + 70 = 200. Returning total = 55 + 70 = 125. Marginal relative frequency = 125/200 = 0.625.

questions.question_text: A university surveys 500 students about housing (on-campus or off-campus) and year (freshman or upperclassman). Results: Freshman & On-campus = 150, Freshman & Off-campus = 50, Upperclassman & On-campus = 100, Upperclassman & Off-campus = 200. Compare the conditional relative frequency of living on-campus for freshmen versus upperclassmen.

options.text: Freshmen: 75% on-campus; Upperclassmen: about 33.3% on-campus
Freshmen: 50% on-campus; Upperclassmen: 50% on-campus
Freshmen: 30% on-campus; Upperclassmen: 67% on-campus
Both groups have equal on-campus rates

questions.explanation: Freshmen: 150/200 = 75% on-campus. Upperclassmen: 100/300 = 33.3% on-campus. Freshmen are much more likely to live on-campus.

questions.question_text: A company surveys 300 employees about work preference (remote or in-office) and role (manager or non-manager). Results: Manager & Remote = 15, Manager & In-office = 35, Non-manager & Remote = 120, Non-manager & In-office = 130. What is the conditional relative frequency of preferring remote work given the employee is a manager?
questions.explanation: Manager total = 15 + 35 = 50. Managers preferring remote = 15. Conditional relative frequency = 15/50 = 0.30 = 30%.

questions.question_text: A school district examines test results (pass or fail) by school type (urban or suburban). Results: Urban & Pass = 180, Urban & Fail = 120, Suburban & Pass = 250, Suburban & Fail = 50. Using conditional relative frequencies, which school type has a higher pass rate?

options.text: Urban, with a 60% pass rate
Suburban, with about an 83.3% pass rate
Both have the same pass rate
Urban, with an 83.3% pass rate

questions.explanation: Urban: 180/300 = 60%. Suburban: 250/300 = 83.3%. Suburban schools have a higher pass rate.

questions.question_text: A survey of 200 adults asks about exercise habits (regular or occasional) and diet (healthy or unhealthy). Results: Regular & Healthy = 70, Regular & Unhealthy = 20, Occasional & Healthy = 40, Occasional & Unhealthy = 70. A health organization claims that people who exercise regularly are more likely to eat healthy. Do the data support this?

options.text: Yes; 77.8% of regular exercisers eat healthy vs. 36.4% of occasional exercisers
No; both groups eat healthy at about the same rate
No; occasional exercisers eat healthier
The data is inconclusive

questions.explanation: Regular exercisers: 70/90 = 77.8% eat healthy. Occasional: 40/110 = 36.4% eat healthy. The data strongly support the claim.

questions.question_text: A retail chain surveys 600 customers about shopping method (online or in-store) and age group (under 40 or 40 and over). Results: Under 40 & Online = 180, Under 40 & In-store = 120, 40+ & Online = 90, 40+ & In-store = 210. What percentage of all customers shop online?
questions.explanation: Total online = 180 + 90 = 270. Marginal relative frequency = 270/600 = 0.45 = 45%.

questions.question_text: A city polls 500 residents about transportation preference (car or bus) and neighborhood (north or south). Results: North & Car = 130, North & Bus = 70, South & Car = 150, South & Bus = 150. Is there an association between neighborhood and transportation preference?

options.text: Yes; north residents prefer car at 65% while south residents prefer car at 50%
No; both neighborhoods have identical preferences
Yes; south residents prefer car at 75%
No association can be determined from this data

questions.explanation: North: 130/200 = 65% prefer car. South: 150/300 = 50% prefer car. The different conditional distributions suggest an association between neighborhood and transportation preference.

questions.question_text: An insurance company examines claims data for 1,000 policyholders by age (under 25 or 25+) and claim status (filed claim or no claim). Results: Under 25 & Claim = 80, Under 25 & No Claim = 120, 25+ & Claim = 200, 25+ & No Claim = 600. Which age group has a higher claim rate, and what are the rates?

options.text: Under 25 at 40%; 25+ at 25%
25+ at 40%; Under 25 at 25%
Both groups have a 30% claim rate
Under 25 at 8%; 25+ at 20%

questions.explanation: Under 25: 80/200 = 40% filed claims. 25+: 200/800 = 25% filed claims. The under-25 group has a higher claim rate.
